Lab3

Jami Greb

2026-03-21

Getting started

library(spdep)
## Warning: package 'spdep' was built under R version 4.4.3
## Loading required package: spData
## Warning: package 'spData' was built under R version 4.4.3
## To access larger datasets in this package, install the spDataLarge
## package with: `install.packages('spDataLarge',
## repos='https://nowosad.github.io/drat/', type='source')`
## Loading required package: sf
## Warning: package 'sf' was built under R version 4.4.3
## Linking to GEOS 3.13.0, GDAL 3.10.1, PROJ 9.5.1; sf_use_s2() is TRUE
library(tidycensus)
## Warning: package 'tidycensus' was built under R version 4.4.3
library(sf)
library(tidyverse)
## Warning: package 'tidyverse' was built under R version 4.4.3
## Warning: package 'ggplot2' was built under R version 4.4.3
## Warning: package 'tidyr' was built under R version 4.4.3
## Warning: package 'readr' was built under R version 4.4.3
## Warning: package 'purrr' was built under R version 4.4.3
## Warning: package 'dplyr' was built under R version 4.4.3
## Warning: package 'stringr' was built under R version 4.4.3
## Warning: package 'forcats' was built under R version 4.4.3
## Warning: package 'lubridate' was built under R version 4.4.3
## ── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
## ✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ readr     2.1.5
## ✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ stringr   1.5.1
## ✔ ggplot2   4.0.2     ✔ tibble    3.2.1
## ✔ lubridate 1.9.4     ✔ tidyr     1.3.2
## ✔ purrr     1.2.1
## ── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
## ✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
## ✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
## ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors
library(tmap)
## Warning: package 'tmap' was built under R version 4.4.3

working through example

options(tigris_use_cache = TRUE)
d.all <- get_acs(geography = "county", table = "B15003", output = "wide", geometry = T, year = 2020)
## Getting data from the 2016-2020 5-year ACS
glimpse(d.all)
## Rows: 3,221
## Columns: 53
## $ GEOID       <chr> "13031", "13121", "13179", "13189", "13213", "13209", "131…
## $ NAME        <chr> "Bulloch County, Georgia", "Fulton County, Georgia", "Libe…

## $ geometry    <MULTIPOLYGON [°]> MULTIPOLYGON (((-82.02684 3..., MULTIPOLYGON …
df.vars <- load_variables(2020, "acs5", cache = T)
glimpse(df.vars)
## Rows: 27,850
## Columns: 4
## $ name      <chr> "B01001A_001", "B01001A_002", "B01001A_003", "B01001A_004", …
## $ label     <chr> "Estimate!!Total:", "Estimate!!Total:!!Male:", "Estimate!!To…
## $ concept   <chr> "SEX BY AGE (WHITE ALONE)", "SEX BY AGE (WHITE ALONE)", "SEX…
## $ geography <chr> "tract", "tract", "tract", "tract", "tract", "tract", "tract…
#finding variable that corresponds to female pop estimate with age 75-79
df.vars %>% dplyr::filter(concept == "SEX BY AGE") %>%
  dplyr::filter(str_detect(label, "Female")) %>%
  dplyr::filter(str_detect(label, "75"))
## # A tibble: 1 × 4
##   name       label                                     concept    geography  
##   <chr>      <chr>                                     <chr>      <chr>      
## 1 B01001_047 Estimate!!Total:!!Female:!!75 to 79 years SEX BY AGE block group
sex.by.age <- get_acs(geography = "county", table = "B01001", output = "wide", geometry = T, year = 2020)
## Getting data from the 2016-2020 5-year ACS
glimpse(sex.by.age)
## Rows: 3,221
## Columns: 101
## $ GEOID       <chr> "13031", "13121", "13179", "13189", "13213", "13209", "131…

## $ geometry    <MULTIPOLYGON [°]> MULTIPOLYGON (((-82.02684 3..., MULTIPOLYGON …
#getting ohio data
ohio <- sex.by.age %>% mutate(., STATEID = stringr::str_sub(GEOID,1,2)) %>% dplyr::filter(STATEID == "39")
glimpse(ohio)
## Rows: 88
## Columns: 102
## $ GEOID       <chr> "39043", "39093", "39061", "39007", "39167", "39123", "390…
## $ NAME        <chr> "Erie County, Ohio", "Lorain County, Ohio", "Hamilton Coun…
## $ B01001_001E <dbl> 74419, 309134, 815790, 97416, 60217, 40557, 32440, 1241475…
## $ B01001_001M <dbl> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, 62…

## $ geometry    <MULTIPOLYGON [°]> MULTIPOLYGON (((-82.73447 4..., MULTIPOLYGON …
## $ STATEID     <chr> "39", "39", "39", "39", "39", "39", "39", "39", "39", "39"…
tmap::tm_shape(ohio) + tm_polygons()

#project
sf::st_crs(ohio)
## Coordinate Reference System:
##   User input: NAD83 
##   wkt:
## GEOGCRS["NAD83",
##     DATUM["North American Datum 1983",
##         ELLIPSOID["GRS 1980",6378137,298.257222101,
##             LENGTHUNIT["metre",1]]],
##     PRIMEM["Greenwich",0,
##         ANGLEUNIT["degree",0.0174532925199433]],
##     CS[ellipsoidal,2],
##         AXIS["latitude",north,
##             ORDER[1],
##             ANGLEUNIT["degree",0.0174532925199433]],
##         AXIS["longitude",east,
##             ORDER[2],
##             ANGLEUNIT["degree",0.0174532925199433]],
##     ID["EPSG",4269]]
ohio.proj <- ohio %>% sf::st_transform(., "ESRI:102010")
tmap::tm_shape(ohio.proj) + tm_polygons()

#making neighborhood
nb <- spdep::poly2nb(ohio.proj, queen = TRUE)
nb
## Neighbour list object:
## Number of regions: 88 
## Number of nonzero links: 460 
## Percentage nonzero weights: 5.940083 
## Average number of links: 5.227273
nb[1] #shows the polygon ids for county 1's neighbors
## [[1]]
## [1]  2 66 72
ohio.proj$NAME[1] #name of county
## [1] "Erie County, Ohio"
nb[[1]] %>% ohio.proj$NAME[.] #basically last two steps together
## [1] "Lorain County, Ohio"   "Sandusky County, Ohio" "Huron County, Ohio"
#assigning weights
lw <- spdep::nb2listw(nb, style = "W", zero.policy = TRUE)
lw$weights[1]
## [[1]]
## [1] 0.3333333 0.3333333 0.3333333
#count of neighbors
neighbors <- attr(lw$weights, "comp")$d
hist(neighbors)

#computing avg neighbor pop of F75 for each county(polygon) aka spatial lagged values
f75 <- lag.listw(lw, ohio.proj$B01001_047E)
f75  
##  [1] 2314.0000 5989.0000 3753.0000 3480.3333  442.7500 3235.6667  917.0000
##  [8] 4302.5000 2263.1667 1022.7500 1684.6667  550.0000 1836.8571 1060.1429
## [15] 4372.0000 1667.8333  912.4000  936.1667 1296.6667 1397.6667 3469.2500
## [22] 2850.1250 7564.5000 4114.4000 2893.2000 8028.2000  941.0000  976.7500
## [29]  970.0000 1186.2000 1838.6667  574.7500 1592.0000 1264.3333 9055.0000
## [36] 2547.6000 3428.8333  864.7500  835.0000 8303.3333 1390.6667  599.7500
## [43]  612.6667  732.8000  929.0000 1100.6667 3039.2857  783.1667 1013.5000
## [50]  945.4000  978.7143  695.0000 3981.2500 6667.0000 5727.1667 1061.0000
## [57]  944.0000 2679.6667  699.5000 3368.3333  987.8000 1271.2500 2881.6667
## [64] 2476.0000 3893.2000 1233.4000 1084.5000  748.8000 3066.0000 3279.0000
## [71] 1492.7143 1798.1429 1710.2857 3305.2857 6629.7500  879.7500 3231.7143
## [78] 2779.5000  585.4286  656.6000 5395.6667  905.3333  840.6667  683.0000
## [85]  771.1667 2057.5714  938.5000  903.5000
#computing Moran's I
moran.test(ohio.proj$B01001_047E, lw)
## 
##  Moran I test under randomisation
## 
## data:  ohio.proj$B01001_047E  
## weights: lw    
## 
## Moran I statistic standard deviate = 3.7197, p-value = 9.971e-05
## alternative hypothesis: greater
## sample estimates:
## Moran I statistic       Expectation          Variance 
##       0.204466841      -0.011494253       0.003370742
#plot
moran.plot(ohio.proj$B01001_047E, lw, zero.policy = TRUE, plot = TRUE)

Tasks

#1. making subset with 5 states: OH, PA, MD, WV, VA (39, 42, 24, 54, 51)
sex.by.age <- get_acs(geography = "county", table = "B01001", output = "wide", geometry = T, year = 2020)
## Getting data from the 2016-2020 5-year ACS
some.states <- sex.by.age %>% mutate(., STATEID = stringr::str_sub(GEOID,1,2)) %>% dplyr::filter(STATEID %in% c("39","42","24","54","51"))
glimpse(some.states)
## Rows: 367
## Columns: 102

## $ B01001_040E <dbl> 635, 546, 913, 16352, 1576, 2723, 892, 315, 556, 83, 1176,…

## $ B01001_042E <dbl> 217, 304, 381, 4792, 648, 948, 310, 268, 420, 35, 534, 623…
## $ B01001_042M <dbl> 89, 89, 135, 440, 146, 178, 101, 114, 136, 24, 144, 142, 7…
## $ B01001_043E <dbl> 639, 263, 544, 6777, 997, 1424, 798, 314, 622, 107, 720, 1…
## $ B01001_043M <dbl> 106, 86, 175, 580, 178, 208, 219, 114, 133, 55, 176, 162, …
## $ B01001_044E <dbl> 267, 169, 379, 3554, 690, 1203, 575, 255, 186, 37, 395, 62…
## $ B01001_044M <dbl> 126, 61, 128, 408, 147, 223, 131, 95, 80, 26, 130, 121, 62…
## $ B01001_045E <dbl> 230, 377, 544, 4998, 1172, 860, 780, 332, 422, 87, 896, 99…
## $ B01001_045M <dbl> 77, 103, 139, 477, 185, 184, 167, 88, 128, 48, 206, 153, 8…
## $ B01001_046E <dbl> 592, 490, 604, 7244, 928, 1580, 1027, 360, 790, 94, 1199, …
## $ B01001_046M <dbl> 134, 121, 159, 465, 186, 221, 201, 87, 144, 38, 248, 168, …
## $ B01001_047E <dbl> 391, 515, 569, 4389, 831, 1088, 626, 357, 500, 133, 749, 9…
## $ B01001_047M <dbl> 139, 97, 127, 366, 133, 184, 140, 80, 145, 76, 159, 111, 5…
## $ B01001_048E <dbl> 250, 125, 322, 3198, 857, 778, 540, 199, 352, 19, 633, 762…
## $ B01001_048M <dbl> 90, 66, 157, 381, 157, 155, 169, 73, 127, 17, 162, 148, 52…
## $ B01001_049E <dbl> 426, 100, 326, 2054, 660, 819, 588, 238, 197, 96, 877, 116…
## $ B01001_049M <dbl> 140, 60, 130, 264, 146, 172, 144, 92, 83, 102, 156, 164, 5…
## $ geometry    <MULTIPOLYGON [°]> MULTIPOLYGON (((-78.69247 3..., MULTIPOLYGON …
## $ STATEID     <chr> "51", "51", "51", "51", "51", "51", "51", "51", "51", "51"…
#project
local.states <- some.states %>% sf::st_transform(., "ESRI:102010")
tmap::tm_shape(local.states) + tm_polygons() #map

#2. choosing a variable: 21 year old females
df.vars <- load_variables(2020, "acs5", cache = T)
df.vars
## # A tibble: 27,850 × 4
##    name        label                                    concept        geography
##    <chr>       <chr>                                    <chr>          <chr>    
##  1 B01001A_001 Estimate!!Total:                         SEX BY AGE (W… tract    
##  2 B01001A_002 Estimate!!Total:!!Male:                  SEX BY AGE (W… tract    
##  3 B01001A_003 Estimate!!Total:!!Male:!!Under 5 years   SEX BY AGE (W… tract    
##  4 B01001A_004 Estimate!!Total:!!Male:!!5 to 9 years    SEX BY AGE (W… tract    
##  5 B01001A_005 Estimate!!Total:!!Male:!!10 to 14 years  SEX BY AGE (W… tract    
##  6 B01001A_006 Estimate!!Total:!!Male:!!15 to 17 years  SEX BY AGE (W… tract    
##  7 B01001A_007 Estimate!!Total:!!Male:!!18 and 19 years SEX BY AGE (W… tract    
##  8 B01001A_008 Estimate!!Total:!!Male:!!20 to 24 years  SEX BY AGE (W… tract    
##  9 B01001A_009 Estimate!!Total:!!Male:!!25 to 29 years  SEX BY AGE (W… tract    
## 10 B01001A_010 Estimate!!Total:!!Male:!!30 to 34 years  SEX BY AGE (W… tract    
## # ℹ 27,840 more rows
df.vars %>% dplyr::filter(concept == "SEX BY AGE") %>%
  dplyr::filter(str_detect(label, "Female")) %>%
  dplyr::filter(str_detect(label, "21"))
## # A tibble: 1 × 4
##   name       label                               concept    geography  
##   <chr>      <chr>                               <chr>      <chr>      
## 1 B01001_033 Estimate!!Total:!!Female:!!21 years SEX BY AGE block group
#3. visualize the variable with histogram
ggplot(local.states, aes(x=B01001_033E))+
  geom_histogram(color="black", fill = "magenta3")+
  theme_minimal() + labs(title ="Population size of 21 year old females across counties", subtitle = "in OH, PA, MD, WV, & VA" , x = "Number of 21 year old females", y = "Number of counties")
## `stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.

#4. Choropleth map of proportion of 21 year old females (033) to total female population (026)
#normalizing
local.states2 <- local.states %>% mutate(., f21prop = B01001_033E/B01001_026E)
#mapping
tm_shape(local.states2) + tm_polygons(fill = "f21prop")

#5. Developing contiguity-based spatial weights matrix (queen)
#row-standardize the W
nbh <- spdep::poly2nb(local.states2, queen = TRUE)
w <- spdep::nb2listw(nbh, style = "W", zero.policy = TRUE)
w$weights[1]
## [[1]]
## [1] 0.1428571 0.1428571 0.1428571 0.1428571 0.1428571 0.1428571 0.1428571
#plot histogram of number of neighbors
Qneighbors <- attr(w$weights, "comp")$d #counts
hist(Qneighbors)

#calculate average number of neighbors
mean(Qneighbors)
## [1] 5.19891
#make Moran plot
moran.plot(local.states2$B01001_033E, w, zero.policy = TRUE, plot = TRUE)

#6. Repeat with IDW method
#assign weights
nbh <- spdep::poly2nb(local.states2, queen = TRUE)
idw <- spdep::nb2listwdist(nbh, sex.by.age, type = "idw", zero.policy = TRUE)
idw$weights[1]
## [[1]]
## [1] 0.39290915 0.02498205 0.05799536 0.03241386 0.08638196 0.07859044 0.02673869
#plot histogram of number of neighbors
idwneighbors <- attr(w$weights, "comp")$d
hist(idwneighbors)

#calculate average number of neighbors
mean(idwneighbors)
## [1] 5.19891
#make Moran plot
moran.plot(local.states2$B01001_033E, idw, zero.policy = TRUE, plot = TRUE)

##Questions

  1. Moran’s I is calculated by subtracting values of two nearby locations by the average of all locations and multitplying them to evaluate of locations near each other have values similar to each other.

  2. A spatially-lagged variable is a variable that changed more the farther away it is from a location.

  3. The Moran plot for the idw method appears to have a stronger positive autocorrelation. It is apparent that the chosen method alters the extent of the outcome, which makes sense as in the first method, all neighbors were simply neighbors, whereas in the second method, some neighbors were more “neighbor-y” than others. This means that the neighbors valued as closer would be expected to have a more similar variable value than the other, which directly affects the spatial autocorrelation analysis.

  4. An observation in the “H-L” quadrant has an above average value but is surrounded by values below average and is therefore likely an outlier (which is why looking in the “H-L” quadrant is useful, because you want to detect outliers.

##Bonus

#finding spatial lag
f21 <- lag.listw(w, local.states2$B01001_033E)
#plotting
ggplot(local.states2, aes(x=B01001_033E, y=f21)) +
  geom_point() + geom_abline() + theme_minimal()

#I think this code is the first step to testing if observations are significant
moran.test(local.states2$B01001_033E, w)
## 
##  Moran I test under randomisation
## 
## data:  local.states2$B01001_033E  
## weights: w    
## 
## Moran I statistic standard deviate = 11.271, p-value < 2.2e-16
## alternative hypothesis: greater
## sample estimates:
## Moran I statistic       Expectation          Variance 
##        0.37261195       -0.00273224        0.00110895
#but that's as far as I got
